skill.name: wechat-article-search-api-skill
skill.description: Search WeChat articles through the BrowserAct API, retrieve full article content, and return structured results for research, monitoring, and analysis.
skill.summary: Search WeChat articles and retrieve full content through BrowserAct.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported network access, environment variable access, command examples, and a critical heuristic combination. Review confirmed a legitimate BrowserAct API integration that sends user search parameters and a bearer token to a third-party service, while the command and weak-crypto signals are false positives from documentation text. No prompt injection, obfuscation, arbitrary command execution, or confirmed malicious intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party API Data Transfer
medium_findings.description: The script posts user-provided keywords, limit, and publication date to the BrowserAct workflow API, then polls and retrieves task results. This appears necessary for the skill, but users should understand that search terms and returned article data are processed by a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly sends workflow input parameters to api.browseract.com and later retrieves task output. This is clear third-party processing, though it matches the stated purpose of the skill.

medium_findings.title: Bearer API Key Use in Network Requests
medium_findings.description: The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the environment and places it in an Authorization bearer header for API requests. This is a legitimate authentication pattern, but it requires careful secret handling and user consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment access and Authorization header construction are explicit in the script. I did not find evidence that the key is logged or sent anywhere except the declared BrowserAct API.

low_findings.title: Documented Shell Invocation Example
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes a bash example for running the Python script. The static command-execution findings are mostly false positives from Markdown formatting and parameter documentation, not hidden command execution inside code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only actual command context is a visible Markdown invocation example. It does not execute automatically and no shell metacharacter construction was found in the Python script.

low_findings.title: False Positive Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography and reconnaissance alerts appear to be keyword matches against words such as API, AI, IP, output, and contains. No cryptographic operations, system enumeration, or prompt injection text was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found ordinary documentation and task ID handling at the flagged areas. I found no hash functions, encryption APIs, host reconnaissance, or instruction override language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Workflow Execution with Secret Header
dangerous_patterns.description: The script combines external network calls with environment-sourced API credentials. This pattern is acceptable for an API integration but should remain disclosed because it moves user queries through BrowserAct infrastructure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combined network and credential pattern is directly present. The surrounding context supports legitimate API use rather than malicious exfiltration.

content.value_statement: WeChat article research is difficult when teams need full text, dates, authors, and source links at scale. This skill runs a BrowserAct workflow that searches by keyword and returns structured article content for analysis.
actual_capabilities: Runs a Python script that calls the BrowserAct WeChat Article Search workflow API.
Accepts search keywords, an article limit, and an optional publication date filter.
Reads the BrowserAct API key from the BROWSERACT_API_KEY environment variable.
Starts a BrowserAct task, polls status, and retrieves final workflow output.
Prints article data such as title, URL, author, publication date, image URL, and body content.
limitations: Requires a valid BrowserAct API key before any search can run.
Depends on BrowserAct service availability and workflow template behavior.
Can take several minutes because the script polls asynchronous task status.
Does not validate article accuracy beyond the data returned by the BrowserAct API.

use_cases.title: Monitor brand mentions
use_cases.description: Track WeChat articles that mention a brand, product, campaign, or executive name.

use_cases.title: Research industry topics
use_cases.description: Collect full WeChat articles about a market trend for summaries and qualitative analysis.

use_cases.title: Review competitor updates
use_cases.description: Search for competitor-related posts and compare publication timing, authors, and article themes.

prompt_templates.title: Search a simple topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Search WeChat articles for "electric vehicles" and return up to 5 results.

prompt_templates.title: Filter by date
prompt_templates.prompt: Search WeChat articles for "AI agent" with a limit of 10 and the publication date filter "2026-03-11".

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a monitoring summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Find WeChat articles mentioning our product name, then summarize titles, authors, publication dates, and major themes.

prompt_templates.title: Build an analysis dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Search WeChat for three competitor names, collect article metadata and body text, and group results by competitor and topic.

output_examples.input: Search WeChat articles for "AI agent" with a limit of 5.
output: A list of matching WeChat articles with titles and source links.
Publication dates, authors, image links, and available body content.
A short summary of recurring themes across the returned articles.

output_examples.input: Find recent WeChat posts about a competitor product launch.
output: Relevant article entries grouped by publisher or author.
Quoted source URLs for each article so reviewers can verify the result.
Notes on launch claims, product features, and audience reaction signals.

best_practices: Use narrow keywords and a small limit for the first run.
Set BROWSERACT_API_KEY as an environment variable instead of pasting it into prompts.
Review source links before using article content in reports or external decisions.
anti_patterns: Do not search for sensitive personal data without a lawful and approved purpose.
Do not assume BrowserAct results are complete coverage of all WeChat content.
Do not retry repeatedly when authorization errors show the API key is invalid.

faq.question: What API key does this skill need?
faq.answer: It needs a BrowserAct API key stored as BROWSERACT_API_KEY in the environment.

faq.question: Does the skill scrape WeChat directly?
faq.answer: No. It calls the BrowserAct workflow API, which performs the article search and extraction.

faq.question: What input can I provide?
faq.answer: You can provide keywords, a maximum article count, and an optional publication date filter.

faq.question: What data can the output include?
faq.answer: The output can include article URL, title, author, publication date, image URL, and body content.

faq.question: Why can searches take several minutes?
faq.answer: The script starts an asynchronous BrowserAct workflow and polls until the task finishes or times out.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because it sends queries and an API token to BrowserAct.
